LZPxKI5YGAk.text: Hello and welcome. I'm Captain Mike Bland, the commanding officer of media relations division with the Los Angeles Police Department. This critical incident community briefing is intended to provide you with information about an officer involved shooting that occurred in the city of Los Angeles involving officers from both Hollandbeck and Newton divisions. You're about to see relevant video footage and learn about other evidence and police procedures related to the case so you can have a better understanding of what occurred based on what we know right now. The LAPD conducts very thorough use of force investigations which typically require investigators to interview multiple witnesses, view countless hours of video footage, and analyze a considerable amount of forensic evidence. We are still at the very early stages of this investigation, which can often take up to a year to complete. and our understanding of the incident may change as this additional evidence is collected, analyzed, and reviewed. We also do not draw any conclusions about whether the officers acted consistent with our policies in the law until all the facts are known and the investigation is complete. A word of caution, the images and information you are about to see may be disturbing. When a police officer uses force to arrest a suspect or defend against an attack, it can be graphic and difficult to watch. In addition, there may be strong language used by those shown in the video. As such, viewer discretion is advised, especially for young children and sensitive viewers. On June 9th, 2026, at around 1:45 p.m., Newton Patrol Division officers were conducting patrol in the area of Washington Boulevard in Alama Street when they saw pedestrians standing in the roadway adjacent to the driver's side door of a parked gray Toyota Tacoma pickup truck. As the officers approached and exited their police vehicle to investigate, they saw that the driver of the Tacoma was in possession of a handgun. When the officers attempted to detain him, the driver fled in the Tacoma at a high rate of speed. The driver of the Toyota Tacoma was later identified as Marco Flores. A portion of this incident was captured on the responding officer's digital incar video. Most Mark vehicles assigned to patrol have a camera mounted inside which activates when the emergency lights are turned on. The camera can also be manually activated. These digital incar cameras have a buffer of video without audio. Upon activation, the camera goes back 1 minute and starts recording. 159. We're going to be heading eastbound Washington. A portion of this incident was also captured on the responding officer's bodywn video. Bodywn video cameras are used by most officers assigned to field duties. They are worn at chest level and capture a general perspective within line of sight from that angle. The angle of the camera prohibits viewers from seeing everything the officer saw and experienced. Upon activation, both audio and video will turn on. However, bodywn video cameras have a buffer of video without audio from the previous 2 minutes prior to activation. This feature is designed to capture incidents that occur suddenly where an officer doesn't immediately activate the camera. Here is bodywn video from the officers involved in this incident. You see the van? >> No, I don't to be honest. I heard you do. All right. >> We're going to be heading eastbound Washington. Go for it. Go for it. We got pursuit. The officers requested the assistance of additional patrol units, an air unit, and a supervisor, and pursued the Tacoma. Flores accelerated away in an apparent attempt to evade, and soon after, the pursuing officers lost sight of the Tacoma in the area of South Sodto Street and East Washington Boulevard and subsequently terminated the pursuit. The officers provided the vehicle description of the Tacoma, a description of Flores, and the last known direction of travel to responding units. >> I think he just went right now. Approximately 1 minute later and a short distance away, the pursuing officers located the Tacoma traveling southbound on Sodto Street from 26th Street, reactivated their police vehicles emergency lights and siren, and re-engaged the vehicle pursuit. Flores continued driving erratically at a high rate of speed for several more miles through a commercial district as the pursuit eventually entered Hollandbeck area. After several minutes, the pursuit continued through a residential neighborhood and the pursuing officers transitioned into tracking mode. >> Initiate tracking high speed. As the air unit tracked Flores, he refused to slow down and eventually entered the northbound 101 freeway on-ramp at Uclid Avenue. Flores continued on the freeway for several more miles before exiting on Olympic Boulevard in central area. He continued driving erratically at a high rate of speed and ultimately returned to a residential neighborhood of Hollandbeck area. Moments later, as he was entering the northbound 101 freeway on-ramp once more, Flores abruptly negotiated a U-turn and traversed down a dirt path back onto Marietta Street. Tracking of Flores continued for several more minutes before responding officers re-engaged in the pursuit eastbound Gerado Street from South M Street. The vehicle pursuit continued for several more miles as Flores continued westbound 8th Street at a high rate of speed. When Flores attempted to negotiate a westbound turn on a Garnett Street from northbound Commulo Street, he crashed into a fence. >> Garnet TC, >> get your back. Get your back. Get your back. His white shirt, blue pants, shoes. He has the green westbound white stripes. Following the traffic crash, Flores exited the Tacoma and fled from the officers. During the ensuing foot pursuit, Flores produced the handgun and fired at the pursuing officers, resulting in an officer involved shooting. We got him. >> We got him. GUN GUN. EVERYTHING HAS running west blue pants. Ready. Keep on shot fired. We're going to be three right up here. Oh [ __ ] Shots fired. We're going to be right up. Here is additional bodywn video from the officers involved in this incident. on the GROUND RIGHT NOW. I'M GOING TO GET YOU. >> HEY, DON'T REACH. DON'T REACH. DON'T REACH. DON'T REACH. Don't reach. Don't reach. >> Stop reaching. GO. HEAT. HEAT. STOP. STOP. STOP. [screaming] GO. GUN, GUN, GUN, GUN. for gun Good. Ahead. drop down right fire. He's right here. >> Oh [ __ ] >> Hey, get out. Get in the back. >> A portion of this incident was also captured by news media aerial footage. Flores was struck by gunfire, fell to the ground, and dropped the handgun. The officers approached Flores and took him into custody without further incident. Officers then place Flores into the recovery position until paramedics arrived. During the incident, one of the pursuing officers was struck in the chest by gunfire from Flores. Although the round did not penetrate the officer's ballistic vest, he sustained injuries that were non-lifethreatening. The officer was transported to a local hospital for treatment and later released. Two police vehicles involved in this incident also sustained impacts from Flores gunfire. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics responded and transported Flores to a local hospital where he was treated for multiple gunshot wounds he sustained. While there, Flores underwent surgery and is currently listed in critical but stable condition. He is expected to survive. Investigators recovered a 9mm semi-automatic Israel Military Industries Baby Desert Eagle handgun that Flores was armed with from the scene and booked it as evidence. Investigators also collected 17 discharged cartridge cases believed to have been fired by Flores from his handgun and booked them as evidence. Investigators later determined that the Toyota Tacoma Flores was driving had been reported stolen. Flores was absentee booked for attempted murder on a police officer. On June 15th, 2026, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following charges against Flores. Four counts of attempted murder on a police officer. Seven counts of assault with a deadly weapon, semi-automatic firearm, on a police officer. Three counts of assault with a deadly weapon, semi-automatic firearm on a person. One count of felon in possession of a firearm. and one count of felony evading. Marco Flores is a 35-year-old resident of Los Angeles. As part of the comprehensive evaluation of this incident, a pursuit investigation was initiated. The pursuit investigation will be reviewed by the department to determine the involved officer's adherence to the law and department policy. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests as new evidence is found. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing, and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. Additionally, the Office of the Inspector General will conduct a parallel and independent review of the investigation and will provide its recommendations to the board. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious use of force incidents, I encourage you to visit LAPDonline.org where you can find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

While there, and despite life-saving efforts by medical staff, Gonzalez was pronounced deceased. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics also transported both involved officers to a local hospital to be treated for the multiple injuries they sustained during the violent confrontation with Gonzalez. Force Investigation Division assumed responsibility for this investigation and responded to the scene. Representatives from the California Department of Justice also responded to the scene and will be conducting an independent investigation of this incident. Lawrence Gonzalez was a 39-year-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests as new evidence is found. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the Civilian Board of Police Commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing, and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. Additionally, the Office of the Inspector General will conduct a parallel and independent review of the investigation and will provide its recommendations to the board. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious use of force incidents, I encourage you to visit LAPDonline.org, or where you can find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

A short time after their arrival, a crisis negotiation team attempted to establish communication with Witsie. However, he continued to not comply. The canine officers then deployed the dog, which resulted in a subsequent canine bite. Here is additional body-worn video from one of the officers involved in the incident. Witsie was subsequently taken into custody without further incident. After being taken into custody, the officers discovered that Witsie had not been struck by gunfire but had sustained visible injuries as a result of the canine contact. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics responded and transported Witsie to a local hospital to be treated for the wounds he sustained as a result of the 40 mm foam projectile round strike and the canine contact. Investigators recovered Witsie's 9 mm Smith & Wesson semi-automatic pistol from the officers at scene and booked it as evidence. On November 21st, 2025, Witsie was medically cleared for booking and was arrested for assault with a deadly weapon on a peace officer. On November 25th, 2025, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following charges against Witsie. Four counts of assault with a deadly weapon with semi-automatic firearm on a peace officer. Two counts of assault with a deadly weapon with semi-automatic firearm. And one count of felon in possession of a firearm. Anthony Witsie is a 54-year-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests as new evidence is found. After the investigation is complete, our Critical Incident Review Division will forward their findings to the Chief of Police, who will make his recommendation to the Civilian Board of Police Commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. Additionally, the Office of the Inspector General will conduct a parallel and independent review of the investigation and will provide its recommendations to the board. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious use of force incidents, I encourage you to visit LAPDonline.org, where you can find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this Critical Incident Community Briefing.

Upon arrival to the location of the radio call, the officers spoke with the victim who provided details of the incident. The officers walked out of the location and returned to their police vehicle to report the vandalism. A short time later, Alva crossed the street and approached the officers holding a combined drywall hammer and hatchet. Alva began to argue with the officers and eventually walked across the street to the west sidewalk after becoming increasingly agitated. The officers then requested the assistance of an air unit, additional patrol units, and a supervisor for a 415 man. Olva continued to act erratically and yell while across the street as additional units arrived. A short time later, notifications were made to the mental evaluation unit, who provided advisement on the incident. The officers continued to give Alva numerous commands to stop, get out of the street, and drop the hammer. However, he did not comply and crossed the street towards the officers, still holding the hammer. As Alva drew closer, officers discharged less lethal munitions, including a taser, 40 millimeter less lethal launchers, and a beanag shotgun. All of which were ineffective at stopping Alva. Still armed with the hammer, Alva advanced towards an officer, at which point an officer involved shooting occurred. Alva was struck by gunfire and fell to the ground. Additionally, one of the officer's rounds struck the handle of the hammer Alva was holding. The officers took Alva into custody without further incident and requested a rescue ambulance. A taser is an electronic control device that is carried by nearly all patrol officers. It fires two metal probes that are designed to cause neuromuscular incapacitation. The 40mm less lethal device fires a foam projectile round. The round strikes the person's body and is intended to cause pain. While there may be bruising, it is designed to distribute energy over a broad surface area and not penetrate the body. The primary target for this less lethal device is the naval or belt line. The bean bag shotgun is a Remington 870 shotgun which has been configured with a green slide handle and stock rifle barrel and side saddle ammunition holder. The color green is used to identify that the shotgun is for the sock round only, not lethal munitions and designed to fire a cloth sock filled with small metal pellets. The primary target area is the naval area or belt line. Officers may target the suspect's arms, hands, or legs when practicable. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics responded and transported Alva to a local hospital where he was admitted for the gunshot wounds he sustained during the incident. No officers or other community members were injured as a result of this incident. Investigators recovered the combined drywall hammer and hatchet. Olva was armed with a screwdriver and a three and a half inch folding knife at scene and booked them as evidence. Olva was arrested and absentee booked for assault with a deadly weapon on a peace officer. On December 4th, 2023, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following felony charges against Alva. One count of assault with a deadly weapon on a police officer. One count of criminal threats. One count of exhibiting a deadly weapon to a police officer to resist arrest. The Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office also filed the following misdemeanor charge against Oliva. One count of vandalism. Francisco Javier Oliva is a 53-year-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics drawing and exhibiting a weapon and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all Los Angeles police officers. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious uses of force, visit LAPDonline.org where you can also find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

The officers announced their presence and gave Gerrio repeated verbal commands to show his hands. The officers saw that Gerrio was holding a large kitchen knife in his right hand and repeatedly ordered him to drop the knife. Gerredo did not comply with the officer's commands and instead continued walking toward the officers while still holding the knife and yelling obscenities. As Gerrio began to descend the stairwell and neared the officers, an officer involved shooting occurred. Gerrio collapsed in the stairwell and ultimately came to rest on the stairwell landing. Pp-QE7N-4LE.text: Hello, my name is Captain Kelly Monise, commanding officer of media relations division of the Los Angeles Police Department. This critical incident community briefing is intended to provide you with information about an officer involved shooting that occurred in Olympic division in the city of Los Angeles on August 6th, 2023 at around 12:15 p.m. You are about to see relevant video footage and learn about other evidence and police procedures related to the case so you can have a better understanding of what occurred based on what we know right now. The LAPD conducts very thorough use of force investigations, which typically require investigators to interview multiple witnesses, view numerous hours of video footage, and analyze a significant amount of forensic evidence. We are still at the very early stages of this investigation, which can often take up to a year to complete, and our understanding of the incident may change as this additional evidence is collected, analyzed, and reviewed. We also do not draw any conclusions about whether the officers acted consistent with our policies in the law until all the facts are known and the investigation is complete. A word of caution. The images and information you're about to see may be disturbing. When a police officer uses force to arrest a suspect or defend against an attack, it can be graphic and difficult to watch. In addition, there may be strong language used by those shown in the video. Viewer discretion is advised, especially for young children and sensitive viewers. Olympic patrol officers were traveling eastbound on the 2600 block of Pico Boulevard when they saw a suspect standing in the opposing lanes of traffic pointing a handgun in their direction. That suspect was later identified as Jose Delgado. As Delgado fired at the officers, they stopped their vehicle in the roadway, resulting in an officer involved shooting. Here is the corresponding radio broadcast. Officer, any shots fired. Shots fired. We're going to be at Pico Pico and Kenmore. Suspect male Hispanic running eastbound on Pico Boulevard. Male Hispanic with a with a gray tank top. Delgado ran away and discarded his handgun near the intersection of Pico Boulevard and Dwey Avenue. As additional officers arrived in the area, they were directed to Delgato's last known location. The officers conducted a search for Delgato in the surrounding neighborhood and located him a few minutes later near Pico Boulevard and Barendo Street. As the officers attempted to detain Delgado, he raised his fists and attempted to strike one officer, which resulted in a subsequent taser deployment. Delgado was struck by the taser, fell to the ground, and was taken into custody without further incident. It was later determined that Delgado had been struck by gunfire. A taser is an electronic control device that is carried by nearly all patrol officers. It fires four metal probes that are designed to cause neuromuscular incapacitation. A portion of this incident was captured by a witness on their dash cam video. a portion. of this incident was also captured on the officer's bodywn video. Bodywn video cameras are used by most officers assigned to field duties. They are worn at chest level and capture a general perspective within line of sight from that angle. The angle of the camera prohibits viewers from seeing everything the officers saw and experienced. Upon activation, both audio and video will turn on. However, bodywn video cameras have a buffer of video without audio from the previous 2 minutes prior to activation. This feature is designed to capture incidents that occur suddenly when an officer doesn't immediately activate the camera. Here is bodywn video from the officers involved in this incident. Get down. Come on. SCR [ __ ] arms. Put me down there, homie. Put me down. You can't put me down. You can't. I can't breathe. I can't breathe. I can't breathe. It's not. You should stop. Okay. It looks like we have here. [Music] Hey guys, you have amazing [Music] custody. Do we still need the perimeter and additional unit? Hey, this verifies as well as him. I just want to make sure. Yeah. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics transported Delgado to a local hospital where he was treated for the gunshot wounds he sustained during the incident and was ultimately cleared for booking. He was subsequently arrested for attempted murder. Investigators determined that the police vehicle the officers were driving was struck by gunfire. However, no officers or community members were injured during the incident. Investigators recovered Delgato's semi-automatic pistol at scene and booked it as evidence. Investigators learned that the pistol was a ghost gun. Ghost guns are firearms manufactured without serial numbers and require no background checks for purchase, making them untraceable to law enforcement authorities. Ghost guns are generally homemade from polymer plastic or metal that need minor modifications to make them operable. On August 8th, 2023, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following charges against Elgato. Two counts of attempted murder. Two counts of an assault with a deadly weapon upon a police officer. One count of shooting at an occupied vehicle. One count of felon in possession of a firearm. And one count of possession of ammunition by a prohibited possessor. Jose Delgado is a 29-year-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious uses of force, visit LAPDonline.org, where you can also find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

L19ITDp9Sfw.text: Hello, I'm Captain Stacy Spell, commanding officer of media relations division with the Los Angeles Police Department. This critical incident community briefing is intended to provide you with information about an officer involved shooting that occurred in the unincorporated area of Los Angeles County. The Los Angeles Police Department critical incident video release policy requires that the department release relevant video footage from all officer involved shootings, significant uses of force, and in custody deaths within 45 days. However, this incident occurred outside the city limits while the officer was off duty. Therefore, the Los Angeles Police Department only has jurisdiction over the administrative investigation. The Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department is handling the criminal aspect of this incident, and all evidence, including any photographic and video evidence, is the property of the Sheriff's Department. The purpose of this briefing is to provide the latest information and evidence we have collected related to this case, so you can have a better understanding of what occurred based on what we know right now. The LAPD conducts very thorough use of force investigations, which typically require investigators to interview multiple witnesses, view numerous hours of video footage, and analyze a significant amount of forensic evidence. We're still at the very early stages of this investigation, which can often take up to a year to complete, and our understanding of the incident may change as this additional evidence is collected, analyzed, and reviewed. On January 10th, 2022, at around 9:15 p.m., an off-duty LAPD officer was looking at a home for sale with his girlfriend in the 1700 block of East 87th Street. While at the location, three male Hispanic suspects approached the officer and his girlfriend and attempted to rob them at gunpoint. During the attempted robbery, an officer involved shooting occurred. The suspects fled the scene in a black pickup truck. Los Angeles County Sheriff's deputies responded to the shooting call and discovered the LAPD officer who had been shot. They immediately transported the injured officer to a nearby hospital. Unfortunately, while at the hospital, our officer died from his injuries. That LAPD officer was identified as Fernando Uriel Aoyos. Officer Aroyos was a three-year veteran of the LAPD and was last assigned to Olympic Area. Los Angeles Sheriff's Department deputies received an additional radio call of a man with a gunshot wound near the area of the incident. They responded and located a suspect who had been involved in this incident. That suspect was identified as Ernesto Cisnerto. Cisnertos was taken into custody and transported to a local hospital with non-life-threatening gunshot wounds sustained during the incident. Los Angeles Sheriff's Department homicide Bureau responded to the scene and assumed investigative responsibility for the incident. On January 12th, 2022, three additional suspects were located and apprehended. Those suspects were identified as Luis Alfredo Dear Rosa Rios, Jesse Contras, and Haley Marie Gisham. On January 13th, 2022, the United States Attorney's Office filed a criminal complaint charging violent crime in aid of raketeering by car on Rios, Cisneros, Contraras, and Gisham. Officer Aoyo's weapon was recovered at scene by deputies and booked as evidence. An additional handgun was also recovered at scene by deputies and booked as evidence. Ernesto Cisneros is a 22-year-old resident of Los Angeles. Luis Alfredo de la Rosa Rios is a 29-year-old resident of Los Angeles. Jesse Contraros is a 34year-old resident of Los Angeles. Haley Marie Gisham is a 18-year-old resident of Los Angeles. Investigators from the LAPD's force investigation division responded to the scene and initiated an administrative investigation. The Los Angeles Police Department is fully cooperating with the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department in their criminal investigation. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the Civilian Board of Police Commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing, and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious uses of force, visit LAPDonline.org, where you can also find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

The Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics responded to the scene and transported the injured officer to a local hospital where he received treatment for a significant injury to his left hand. The officer was released from the hospital but later required surgery. Morales was also transported to a local hospital for evaluation. was immediately cleared for booking and he was transported to an LAPD jail facility. Investigators recovered Morales's crossbow, the arrow that struck the officer, and a 30 caliber pallet rifle at the scene and booked those items as evidence. On June 10th, 2020, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following counts against Morales. one count of attempt murder on a police officer, two counts of assault with a deadly weapon on a police officer, and three counts of shooting into an inhabited dwelling. Gonzalo Morales is a resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. They'll continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is completed, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. That board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing and exhibiting of a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all LAPD officers. If you'd like more information on how the LAPD and the LA District Attorney's Office investigates all officer involved shootings and other serious uses of force, visit lapdonline.org. org where you can also find LAPD's use of force policies and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

However, Henderson did not comply with the officer's commands and instead turned and began to walk away. As Henderson neared the end of the apartment complex walkway, he turned toward the officers while still holding both weapons, resulting in an officer involved shooting. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics transported Henderson to a local hospital where he was treated by medical staff for the gunshot wound he sustained during the incident. There were no officers or community members injured during this incident. Additionally, the officers were unable to identify any victims related to an assault with a deadly weapon. Investigators recovered Henderson's shotgun as well as his loaded assault rifle at scene and booked them both as evidence. The investigators learned that the assault rifle was a ghost gun. Ghost guns are firearms manufactured without serial numbers and require no background checks for purchase, making them untraceable to law enforcement authorities. Ghost guns are generally homemade from polymer plastic or metal that need minor modifications to make them operable. On October 2nd, 2023, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed the following charges against Henderson. One count of shooting at an inhabited dwelling, one count of assault with a semi-automatic firearm upon a peace officer, one count of possession of a firearm by a felon, and one count of possession of a bullet containing an explosive agent. Devin Henderson is a 36-year-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police, who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics, drawing and exhibiting a weapon, and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all Los Angeles police officers. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious uses of force, visit LAPDonline.org, where you can find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

This critical incident community briefing is intended to provide you with information about an officer involved shooting that occurred in 77th Division in the city of Los Angeles on June 28th, 2023 at around 12:50 a.m. You're about to see relevant video footage and learn about other evidence and police procedure related to the case so you can have a better understanding of what occurred based on what we know right now. The LAPD conducts very thorough use of force investigations, which typically require investigators to interview multiple witnesses, view numerous hours of video footage, and analyze a significant amount of forensic evidence. We are still at the very early stages of this investigation, which can often take up to a year to complete, and our understanding of the incident may change as this additional evidence is collected, analyzed, and reviewed. We also do not draw any conclusions about whether the officers acted consistent with our policies in the law until all the facts are known and the investigation is complete. A word of caution, the images and information you're about to see may be disturbing. When a police officer uses force to arrest a suspect or defend against an attack, it can be graphic and difficult to watch. In addition, there may be strong language used by those shown in the video. Viewer discretion is advised, especially for young children and sensitive viewers. 77th Division Patrol officers conducted a traffic stop on a black Lexus sedan in a rear parking lot of a business located in the 7200 block of Southwestern Avenue. Here is the corresponding radio broadcast 52 code 6 western and southwest corner. As the officers spoke with the driver of the Lexus, they heard multiple gunshots coming from an adjacent strip mall nearby. The officers turned their attention to the location of the gunshots and saw a man firing a gun. It was determined the man was walking toward an occupied open business while firing multiple rounds from a pistol he was holding. That suspect was later identified as Michael Meadows. The officers immediately engaged Meadows, resulting in an officer involved shooting. Meadows was struck by gunfire, dropped the pistol, and fell to the ground. Upon arrival of additional officers, Meadows was taken into custody without further incident. A portion of this incident was captured on the responding officer's digital incar video. After being taken into custody, the officers immediately requested a rescue ambulance and attempted life-saving measures on Meadows by performing cardoppulmonary resuscitation, also known as CPR. Los Angeles Fire Department paramedics responded shortly thereafter and pronounced Meadows deceased. Investigators recovered Meadow's pistol equipped with a threaded barrel, making it sound suppressor capable, and an empty magazine at scene, and booked them both as evidence. Investigators learned that the pistol was a ghost gun. Ghost guns are firearms manufactured without serial numbers and require no background checks for purchase, making them untraceable to law enforcement authorities. Ghost guns are generally homemade from polymer plastic or metal that need minor modifications to make them operable. Michael Meadows was a 44 yearear-old resident of Los Angeles. In the next several months, the LAPD will continue to investigate and analyze this incident. We will continue interviewing any new witnesses that may come forward and complete any forensic tests. After the investigation is complete, our critical incident review division will forward their findings to the chief of police who will make his recommendation to the civilian board of police commissioners. The board will evaluate the evidence to determine whether the officer's tactics drawing and exhibiting a weapon and use of deadly force in this instance met the high standards expected of all Los Angeles police officers. If you would like more information on how the LAPD investigates all serious uses of force, visit LAPDonline.org where you can find the LAPD's use of force policy and procedures. Thank you for taking the time to view this critical incident community briefing.

Southeast patrol officers were in the 11,400 block of Alvaro Street when they observed a man riding a bicycle who they recognized as a felony warrant suspect wanted for an assault with a deadly weapon. That suspect was identified as Victor Lopez. As the officers approached Lopez to conduct an investigative stop, Lopez got off of his bicycle and ran away. The officers initiated a foot pursuit and chased Lopez through a residential area. While pursuing Lopez, the officers gave him repeated commands to stop running. However, Lopez did not comply and continued running away. During the foot pursuit, Lopez produced a handgun and fired, resulting in an officer involved shooting. Lopez was struck by gunfire, scaled a nearby fence, and continued to run away. The officers lost sight of Lopez, requested the assistance of additional officers, and established a perimeter in the immediate area. Notifications were made to Metropolitan Division K9, who responded shortly thereafter and assisted Southeast officers in formulating a plan to resolve this situation. K9 officers conducted a search of the area and discovered that Lopez had barricaded himself in a nearby business. Since Lopez was barricaded and refusing to come out of the business, notifications were then made to Metropolitan Division. Special weapons and tactics, also known as SWAT. SWAT personnel with the assistance from the crisis negotiation team attempted to communicate with Lopez for several hours working to deescalate the situation. However, Lopez refused to comply. SWAT officers then introduced chemical agents into the business, resulting in Lopez surrendering without further incident. A portion of this incident was captured on the officer's bodywn video. The LAPD conducts very thorough use of force investigations, which typically require investigators to interview multiple witnesses, view numerous hours of video footage, and analyze a significant amount of forensic evidence. Mission Division Patrol officers were in the 9,400 block of Seulva Boulevard when they observed a suspicious vehicle parked inside the parking lot of a local motel. The officers stopped to conduct a possible stolen vehicle investigation. The officers exited their police vehicle and contacted the occupants of the parked vehicle. That driver of the parked vehicle was identified as Vicente Gonzalez. The passenger of the vehicle was identified as Gonzalez's juvenile son. Officers made verbal contact with Gonzalez as they were conducting their investigation. And during this contact, Gonzalez spontaneously fled on foot. As officers gave chase, Gonzalez produced a handgun and pointed it at one of the pursuing officers, at which time an officer involved shooting occurred. Gonzalez continued running while officers established a perimeter and requested the assistance of additional units. Metropolitan Division K9 personnel arrived at scene shortly thereafter to assist mission patrol officers. However, they were unable to locate Gonzalez. On November 1st, 2021, the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office filed one count of attempted murder, one count of assault with it firearm, one count of child endangerment, four counts of felon in possession of a firearm, two counts of possession of methamphetamine for sale, one count of possession of a controlled substance with intent to sell, and one count of possession of an assault weapon on Gonzalez.
